title: Designing an AI-Native Portfolio That Feels Like a Developer Workspace
excerpt: How I redesigned my portfolio into a developer-first experience using lightweight rendering, JSON-driven content, and a terminal-inspired visual language.
content:
# Why this redesign happened

Most portfolios look clean but feel generic. I wanted mine to feel like a real engineering workspace: structured, intentional, and readable under pressure.

## Goals

- Make the interface feel developer-centric without becoming noisy
- Keep every section data-driven from JSON
- Avoid heavy rendering in image-heavy sections
- Keep mobile interactions smooth

## What changed

### 1) Hero with engineering context

Instead of static slogans, I used a cipher text loop controlled from `personal.json`. That lets me tune copy and timing without editing layout code.

### 2) Lightweight evidence cards

For certificates and activities, I replaced thumbnail-heavy grids with text-first cards. The proof image now opens only on click in a modal. This keeps scrolling responsive.

### 3) Local Tailwind build

I removed Tailwind CDN usage and moved to a local CLI pipeline (`tailwindcss@3`) with generated CSS in `/css/output.css`.

## Small implementation pattern I liked

```js
function openEvidenceModal(payload) {
  const modal = document.getElementById('evidence-modal')
  const image = document.getElementById('evidence-image')
  modal.style.display = 'block'
  image.src = payload.image
}
```

## Result

The site now feels more like a product surface than a template, and interaction cost is lower on sections with many records.

---

If you are designing your own portfolio, start by deciding how it should *feel* for developers first, then layer visuals on top of that behavior.
